top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: The Gulf and Ukraine dominate again. Iran fired missiles and drones at Kuwait and Bahrain early Wednesday; a drone hit Kuwait International Airport's terminal, killing one person (an Indian national) and injuring 63, after US forces struck an Iranian site on Qeshm Island. In Ukraine, the death toll from Russia's overnight barrage rose to 22 — including an eight-year-old recovered from Dnipro rubble — as Zelensky pleaded for more air defences. Diplomacy is stalled: Iran is weighing a US proposal to halt the war while Trump insists talks continue.

stories.headline: Iranian drone kills one at Kuwait airport as US strikes Qeshm Island
stories.summary: Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Kuwait, Bahrain and Gulf shipping in the early hours of 3 June. A drone struck the passenger terminal at Kuwait International Airport, killing one person — identified by India's embassy as an Indian national — and injuring at least 63, with seven needing major surgery; the airport's operations were frozen and flights diverted, Kuwait's Foreign Ministry said. The US military said it struck an Iranian military ground-control station on Qeshm Island in response, that all the ballistic missiles failed to hit their targets, that US and Bahraini forces intercepted three missiles aimed at Bahrain and downed multiple drones, and that no US personnel were harmed. The IRGC claimed its 'predetermined targets were destroyed' and warned of a harsher response (claims attributed to CENTCOM, Kuwait's Foreign and Defence Ministries and the IRGC; not independently verified).
stories.source: The National / Al Jazeera / Washington Post

stories.headline: Ukraine toll from Russia's deadliest barrage in weeks rises to 22, including a child in Dnipro
stories.summary: The death toll from Russia's overnight combined missile-and-drone assault on Ukraine rose to at least 22 on 3 June after rescuers recovered more bodies — including that of an eight-year-old boy — from a destroyed apartment block in Dnipro, where officials said 16 were killed; six died in Kyiv and more than 100 were wounded nationwide. Ukraine's Air Force said Russia fired 73 missiles and 656 drones and that air defences shot down or suppressed 642 targets (40 missiles, 602 drones); more than 40,000 people sheltered in Kyiv's subway, the most in years. Russia struck Dnipro again on the afternoon of 3 June, injuring at least three more, as President Zelensky said the scale of the attack showed Moscow would keep hitting cities unless Kyiv received stronger air defences (Ukrainian figures).

stories.headline: Iran weighs US proposal to halt the war as Trump says talks are continuing
stories.summary: More than three months into the US–Israel war on Iran, Reuters reported that Tehran is studying a proposed agreement to halt the fighting but has not communicated with Washington for several days, with two semi-official Iranian agencies saying Iran had stopped engaging mediators about extending the shaky ceasefire. President Trump disputed that characterisation, saying negotiations were going on 'continuously.' The diplomatic stalemate persists even as the latest Gulf strikes widen the conflict, leaving the truce nominal and the path to a deal unclear (accounts attributed to Reuters' Iranian-agency sourcing and to Trump).

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: A policy-and-weather evening locally. URA released its second-half 2026 land-sales programme, putting nine Confirmed List sites up for some 9,200 private homes and launching a Jurong Lake District white site for tender in July as the second CBD takes shape. The NEA forecasts a partly cloudy night with thundery showers over northern and western Singapore on Thursday morning, 26–34°C. In the courts, three Chinese nationals were charged over a S$50,000 Marina Bay Sands robbery after police recalled their Shanghai-bound flight to arrest them.

stories.headline: URA's 2H2026 land-sales plan offers ~9,200 private homes; Jurong Lake District site launches in July
stories.summary: The Urban Redevelopment Authority announced its second-half 2026 Government Land Sales (GLS) programme on 3 June, with nine Confirmed List sites — eight private residential and one white site — that can yield 4,745 private homes (including 735 executive-condominium units) and 83,350 sqm of commercial space, plus 13 Reserve List sites. Across both lists the programme can supply about 9,200 private residential units, 188,100 sqm of commercial gross floor area and 970 hotel rooms. URA said it is sustaining supply at a high level and will launch a white site at Town Hall Link in the Jurong Lake District — Singapore's planned second CBD — for tender in July.

stories.headline: NEA: Partly cloudy tonight, thundery showers over north and west on Thursday
stories.summary: The Meteorological Service Singapore's 24-hour forecast, issued at 5.37pm on 3 June, expects partly cloudy conditions overnight and thundery showers mainly over northern and western Singapore in the late morning and early afternoon of 4 June. Temperatures are forecast between 26°C and 34°C, with light to moderate winds from the south-southwest.

stories.headline: Three Chinese nationals charged over S$50,000 Marina Bay Sands robbery; flight recalled to arrest them
stories.summary: Police said they were alerted at about 7pm on 1 June to a robbery with hurt along Bayfront Avenue, after a 45-year-old woman arranged to exchange S$50,000 in cash for foreign currency with someone she met on a messaging platform. She and her child were locked on a hotel-room balcony and suffered minor injuries. Three Chinese nationals aged 29 to 34 were arrested after authorities recalled their Shanghai-bound aircraft to the gate at Changi Airport, and about S$50,000 was recovered. The three were charged on 3 June with robbery with hurt under Section 394 of the Penal Code.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Escalation is outpacing diplomacy on every front. In the Gulf, US forces struck an Iranian site on Qeshm Island and Iran fired missiles and drones at Kuwait and Bahrain early on 3 June; a drone hit Kuwait's main airport, killing one and injuring 63, while CENTCOM says the missiles failed or were intercepted. Russia launched its deadliest barrage in weeks on Ukraine overnight, killing at least 22, most in Dnipro. In Lebanon, Israel seized the Crusader-era Beaufort Castle and pushed its offensive past the Litani despite an April truce, with strikes continuing; Gaza's ceasefire holds tenuously while phase-two talks stay stuck over Hamas disarmament.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: Russia launched one of its deadliest combined missile-and-drone barrages in weeks overnight into 2 June; Ukraine's Air Force reported 73 missiles and 656 drones (642 intercepted or suppressed), and by 3 June officials said at least 22 were killed — 16 in Dnipro, where an eight-year-old's body was recovered from a destroyed apartment block, and six in Kyiv — with more than 100 wounded. Russia struck Dnipro again on the afternoon of 3 June.

timeline.event: Russia and Ukraine observed a US-brokered three-day 'Victory Day' ceasefire (9–11 May) that included a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner exchange, confirmed by Zelensky and the Kremlin; fighting resumed after it lapsed.

Russia launched a large overnight combined attack on Kyiv; Ukraine's Air Force said roughly 600 drones and 90 missiles were used, including a hypersonic Oreshnik, and Mayor Klitschko reported deaths and dozens injured with damage across the capital (Ukrainian figures).

Ukraine's military said it struck the Saratov oil refinery and other Russian energy and industrial targets overnight; several Russian regional governors reported drone strikes. Ukraine denied a Russian claim that it had targeted the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant (Ukraine's military; Russian regional officials).

The death toll from Russia's overnight barrage rose to at least 22 after rescuers recovered more bodies, including an eight-year-old boy, from a destroyed apartment block in Dnipro (16 killed there) with six dead in Kyiv and more than 100 wounded. Ukraine's Air Force said Russia fired 73 missiles and 656 drones and that 642 targets were downed or suppressed; over 40,000 sheltered in Kyiv's subway. Russia struck Dnipro again that afternoon, injuring at least three more, and Zelensky pressed allies for more air defences (Ukrainian figures).

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: Escalating: early on 3 June US forces struck an Iranian ground-control station on Qeshm Island near the Strait of Hormuz and Iran fired ballistic missiles and drones at Kuwait, Bahrain and Gulf shipping. A drone hit Kuwait International Airport's terminal, killing one person (an Indian national) and injuring at least 63, Kuwait said; the US said all the missiles failed to hit their targets, that three aimed at Bahrain were intercepted and that no US personnel were harmed. Reuters reports Iran is weighing a US proposal to halt the war but has gone quiet with mediators; Trump says talks continue.

timeline.event: The US and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes on Iranian military, nuclear and leadership targets; Iran retaliated with missiles and drones on Israeli cities and US Gulf bases, triggering the wider war and the Strait of Hormuz shipping crisis (accounts attributed to the parties).

Amid failed ceasefire attempts, the US Navy began a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, intercepting vessels transiting Iranian ports; Iran intermittently closed the strait, leaving hundreds of tankers stranded in the Gulf.

CENTCOM said Iran fired two ballistic missiles at US forces in Kuwait and that they were intercepted with no US personnel harmed; Kuwait said its air defences intercepted 'hostile missiles and drones' and diverted flights from Kuwait International Airport (statements from CENTCOM and the Kuwaiti government).

US forces struck an Iranian military ground-control station on Qeshm Island; Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Kuwait, Bahrain and Gulf shipping. A drone hit Kuwait International Airport's passenger terminal, killing one person (identified by India's embassy as an Indian national) and injuring at least 63, freezing airport operations; the US said all the ballistic missiles failed, that three aimed at Bahrain were intercepted and multiple drones downed, and that no US personnel were harmed. The IRGC claimed its targets were destroyed and warned of a harsher response (claims attributed to CENTCOM, Kuwait's ministries and the IRGC; not independently verified).
timeline.source: The National / Al Jazeera / Washington Post

theatres.name: Israel–Lebanon (Hezbollah)
theatres.status: Israel's offensive is deepening despite the April ceasefire: on 31 May Israeli forces captured the Crusader-era Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon — their first time there in 26 years — as Netanyahu ordered troops to push beyond the Litani River. President Trump said on 2 June that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed 'all shooting will stop,' but neither side confirmed it and Israeli strikes continued, leaving any truce nominal and contested.

timeline.event: A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire took effect and was later extended, but Hezbollah disarmament remained unresolved and both sides traded blame for violations.

Lebanese health authorities said Israeli airstrikes killed at least 19 people and wounded dozens across southern Lebanon and south of Beirut — the first strike on the capital in three weeks — as Israel issued sweeping evacuation orders covering Tyre and other areas (casualty figures attributed to Lebanese authorities).

The Israeli military captured the strategic, 12th-century Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh — about 9 miles from the border and overlooking much of southern Lebanon — after days of fighting, as Netanyahu ordered forces to push deeper beyond the Litani River despite the April ceasefire.

Trump said on Truth Social that, via intermediaries, Hezbollah had 'agreed that all shooting will stop'; Lebanon's embassy said Hezbollah accepted a US proposal for a mutual cessation of attacks. Netanyahu said Israel reserved the right to strike and would 'continue to operate as planned' in southern Lebanon, and Israeli strikes and artillery continued the same day, with Lebanese authorities reporting several killed. Neither party formally confirmed a halt to fighting (announcement and casualty claims attributed to the parties; not independently verified).

theatres.name: Israel–Gaza
theatres.status: The October ceasefire holds tenuously; phase two remains stalled over Hamas disarmament and Israeli withdrawal, while periodic Israeli strikes continue and local health officials report more than 900 Palestinians killed since the truce took effect (figure attributed to Gaza health officials, not independently verified).

timeline.event: The US said phase two of the Gaza ceasefire had begun, opening talks on Hamas disarmament and Israeli withdrawal; major challenges remained, with Hamas insisting on Israeli withdrawal alongside any disarmament and Israel and the US insisting demilitarisation come first.

An Israeli strike in Gaza City killed Mohammed Odeh, described as the leader of Hamas's military wing, along with at least four others including family members; Israel's defence minister called Odeh an 'architect' of the 7 October 2023 attack, and Hamas confirmed his death the next day (Israel says it targeted Hamas figures; Palestinian officials said civilians were among the dead).

Negotiations on phase two remained stalled over Hamas disarmament; the Board of Peace said Hamas 'must disarm, not disappear,' while Hamas rejected disarming until Israel fully implements phase one. Local health officials said Israeli strikes had killed more than 900 Palestinians since the ceasefire took effect, which Israel disputes, saying strikes target threats to its forces (casualty totals attributed to Gaza health officials, not independently verified).

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: The headline shift this evening is the DRC–Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola PHEIC: WHO and the US CDC slashed the suspected-case count from over 1,000 to about 116 after testing cleared most as non-Ebola, recasting the outbreak as smaller than first feared. Confirmed cases keep climbing, though — WHO's validated line list stood near 330 with about 49 deaths as of 31 May, and the US CDC's later national tally runs higher — and the International Medical Corps warns the response is still being outpaced. Two slower outbreaks round out the picture: a now-contained Andes hantavirus cruise-ship cluster (13 cases, 3 deaths) with contacts traced across 32 countries, and elevated US measles at 1,983 cases and no deaths.

outbreaks.name: DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: PHEIC since 17 May; suspected-case count sharply revised down after testing, but confirmed cases still rising; no licensed Bundibugyo-specific vaccine or therapeutic, and insecurity in eastern DRC is hampering containment
outbreaks.scope_note: Laboratory-confirmed only, DRC + Uganda combined, per WHO's validated line list as of 31 May (reported 3 June): DRC 321 confirmed / 48 deaths (6 recovered) and Uganda 9 confirmed / 1 death — about 330 confirmed and 49 deaths. WHO and the US CDC slashed DRC's SUSPECTED count from over 1,000 to 116 after testing cleared most as other illnesses; suspected cases are NOT added to the confirmed totals. The US CDC's national line as of 2 June runs higher (DRC 344/60, Uganda 15/1 ≈ 359 confirmed / 61 deaths) — a case-validation and date lag, not a separate outbreak. DRC's Ituri province is the epicentre; a US doctor infected in DRC is counted separately and treated in Germany. The International Medical Corps cautions the response is being outpaced and the true burden may be higher; agency tallies vary as cases are validated.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster (multi-country)
outbreaks.status: Largely contained; all passengers disembarked, contacts monitored across 32 countries during the long incubation window
outbreaks.scope_note: Cases linked to the cruise ship MV Hondius: 11 confirmed + 2 probable = 13, with 3 deaths (2 confirmed, 1 probable; CFR ~23%), per WHO Disease Outbreak News DON604 (published 28 May, data as of 27 May), matching ECDC. Confirmed cases reported in 4 countries; more than 600 contacts (about 53% high-risk) across 32 countries/territories are under monitoring but are NOT counted as cases. Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person spread; WHO rates the global risk low. No newer DON as of 3 June.

outbreaks.name: United States measles
outbreaks.status: Elevated and outbreak-driven; no deaths reported in 2026, with elimination status at risk
outbreaks.scope_note: Confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions, per the US CDC as of 28 May (1,974 among US residents plus 9 among international visitors); 0 deaths reported in 2026. About 93% (1,847) are outbreak-associated across 30 outbreaks this year. US-only figure (CDC updates weekly); part of a wider global measles resurgence.

stories.headline: WHO and CDC slash DRC Ebola suspected-case count to ~116 from over 1,000
stories.summary: WHO said on 3 June that the Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo is far smaller than initially feared, after it and the US CDC cut the official suspected-case count to about 116 from earlier estimates topping 1,000. A WHO spokesperson said most previously-suspected patients were 'cleared out' through testing because they had other illnesses or only a fever. As of 31 May WHO's validated figures put DRC at 321 confirmed cases, 48 deaths and 6 recoveries, with Uganda at 9 confirmed and 1 death. Relief groups urged caution: the International Medical Corps said the outbreak is still 'outpacing the current response' and could take more than six months to control, and there remains no licensed vaccine or treatment for this Ebola species.

stories.headline: US CDC's national Ebola tally runs higher as confirmed cases keep climbing
stories.summary: Even as the suspected count was revised down, confirmed Ebola cases continued to rise. The US CDC's situation summary, dated as of 2 June, lists DRC at 344 confirmed cases and 60 deaths (plus 116 suspected) and Uganda at 15 confirmed and 1 death — roughly 359 confirmed and 61 deaths combined, above WHO's validated 31 May line of about 330 confirmed and 49 deaths. CDC attributes the gap to case validation and timing; the DRC Ministry of Health removed suspected cases on 29 May that were ruled out after testing. A US doctor who treated patients in DRC and tested positive on 17 May is being cared for in Germany and is counted separately from the two-country totals.

stories.headline: Cruise-ship Andes hantavirus cluster holds at 13 cases, 3 deaths as contacts are traced across 32 countries
stories.summary: WHO's Disease Outbreak News DON604 reports the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster at 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths as of 27 May, with confirmed cases in four countries. All passengers have disembarked, and more than 600 contacts — about 53% of them high-risk — are being monitored across 32 countries and territories given the virus's long incubation period. Andes virus is the only hantavirus known to spread person-to-person; WHO rates the global risk low.

stories.headline: US measles tops 1,980 cases for 2026, almost all outbreak-linked
stories.summary: CDC data as of 28 May show 1,983 confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions and 30 outbreaks, with 93% of cases outbreak-associated and no deaths reported this year. Officials warn the sustained, interconnected transmission — among the highest annual totals since US elimination in 2000 — threatens the country's measles elimination status, amid a wider global resurgence.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Newly disclosed flaws lead this cycle. Acer is racing to patch two maximum-severity zero-days in its Wave 7 mesh routers (CVE-2026-49200/49201) that expose credentials and allow a hardcoded-key backdoor, with no fix until end-June. A separate unpatched VS Code zero-day lets one click steal a developer's GitHub token. Active exploitation continues elsewhere: the critical Kirki WordPress flaw (CVE-2026-8206) is being used to hijack admin accounts, Google patched an exploited Android zero-day (CVE-2025-48595), and Belgium's CCB says the CVSS 9.8 Windows Netlogon RCE (CVE-2026-41089) is being exploited against domain controllers — a claim Microsoft disputes. CISA also flagged an actively exploited Oracle WebLogic flaw.

stories.headline: Acer races to patch two maximum-severity zero-days in Wave 7 routers
stories.summary: Acer disclosed two maximum-severity zero-day flaws in its Wave 7 mesh routers (firmware T7c_GBL_1.01.000055 and earlier). CVE-2026-49200, a broken-access-control bug, lets unauthenticated attackers read plaintext credentials via an exposed log file through the web interface; CVE-2026-49201 stems from a hardcoded AES key in the backup-handling binary, letting remote attackers decrypt, alter and re-encrypt system backups to plant a persistent backdoor. No patch is available yet — Acer says fixes are targeted for the end of June and advises disabling remote management or restricting internet access to trusted IPs in the meantime.

stories.headline: Unpatched VS Code zero-day lets one click steal a developer's GitHub token
stories.summary: Researchers disclosed an unpatched zero-day in Visual Studio Code that lets attackers steal a victim's GitHub authentication token in a single click by tricking the user into following a crafted link, with proof-of-concept exploit code released. No CVE has been assigned yet and no fix is available; affected users are advised to clear cookies and local site data for github.dev in their browser as a stop-gap. The flaw is distinct from the recent Nx Console VS Code extension supply-chain breach.

stories.headline: Critical Kirki WordPress plugin flaw actively exploited to hijack admin accounts
stories.summary: An unauthenticated privilege-escalation flaw in the Kirki WordPress plugin (versions up to 6.0.6, with 500,000+ installs) lets attackers abuse a password-reset REST endpoint to obtain reset links for arbitrary accounts, including administrators, enabling full site takeover. Security firm Wordfence reported blocking hundreds of attacks within 24 hours of disclosure. The flaw was patched in version 6.0.7.

stories.headline: Google patches actively exploited Android zero-day among 124 June fixes
stories.summary: Google's June 2026 Android security update fixes 124 vulnerabilities, including CVE-2025-48595, an Android Framework integer-overflow flaw that lets a local attacker escalate privileges with no user interaction and which Google says 'may be under limited, targeted exploitation' — language it uses for confirmed targeted attacks, often tied to commercial spyware or state actors. The bug affects Android 14, 15, 16 and 16 QPR2; patches ship at the 2026-06-01 and 2026-06-05 patch levels.

stories.headline: Windows Netlogon RCE reported exploited against domain controllers; Microsoft disputes the claim
stories.summary: Belgium's Centre for Cybersecurity (CCB) warned that a Netlogon stack-based buffer-overflow flaw, patched in the May 2026 Patch Tuesday, is now being exploited in the wild for unauthenticated remote code execution on domain controllers across all supported Windows Server versions, including Server 2025. Microsoft said it has no evidence supporting CCB's claim but still urges patching given the flaw's severity, and the CVE is not yet in CISA's KEV catalog. Given Netlogon's role in domain authentication, successful exploitation could compromise an entire Active Directory environment (exploitation claim attributed to CCB and not vendor-confirmed).

stories.headline: CISA orders federal agencies to patch actively exploited Oracle WebLogic flaw
stories.summary: CISA added a high-severity Oracle WebLogic Server vulnerability, originally patched in 2024, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation, setting a federal remediation deadline of around 4 June. The remotely exploitable flaw lets an unauthenticated attacker with network access compromise affected WebLogic instances and access their data; private organisations are urged to patch as well.
